name: Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6)
contraindications: Avoid prolonged high-dose self-supplementation due to neuropathy risk
Use medication review when combined with anticonvulsants or levodopa regimens

sources.title: Vitamin B6 Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)

description: Vitamin B6 includes pyridoxine, pyridoxal, pyridoxamine, and phosphate forms. The active coenzyme form is pyridoxal-5'-phosphate, and adult intake guidance is centered on low mg/day RDA levels.
summary: Vitamin B6 (including pyridoxine) is an essential cofactor nutrient for amino-acid and neurotransmitter metabolism; supplementation is mainly for adequacy/deficiency contexts, with caution at high chronic doses.
mechanism: Established with proposed extensions: B6-dependent enzymes support transamination/decarboxylation and neurotransmitter synthesis (including serotonin and GABA pathways); symptom-specific benefits outside deficiency correction remain mixed.
safetyNotes: RDA-level intake is generally well tolerated.
Chronic high supplemental intake (commonly >100 mg/day) can cause sensory peripheral neuropathy; improvement may occur after stopping.
interactions: High-dose B6 can affect certain anticonvulsants (for example phenytoin).
Can alter levodopa response when levodopa is not co-administered with a decarboxylase inhibitor.
dosage: Supplement studies often use 50–100 mg/day in selected contexts, but prolonged high dosing increases neuropathy risk.
Adult upper intake level context: about 100 mg/day.
preparation: Commonly available as pyridoxine hydrochloride in single-ingredient and B-complex products.
regulatoryStatus: Essential vitamin with established dietary reference intakes; marketed as a dietary supplement ingredient.
